cards.title: Developmental Milestones — Birth to 5 Years
cards._legacyHtml:
AgeGross MotorFine MotorLanguageSocial / Cognitive
6 weeksHead control — raises chin proneGrasp reflex presentStartles to sound, coosSocial smile ★
3 monthsHead steady, no head lag when pulled to sitHands open, holds rattleCoos, vocalises, turns to voiceRecognises parents, smiles spontaneously
6 monthsSits with support, rolls front to backPalmar grasp, transfers objectsBabbles (monosyllables: ba, da)Stranger awareness begins, laughs
9 monthsSits unsupported, pulls to standPincer grasp developing, bangs objectsMama/dada non-specific, waves bye-byeObject permanence, separation anxiety
12 monthsWalks with one hand held, stands alone brieflyNeat pincer grasp ★, releases voluntarily1–2 words with meaning, mama/dada specificWaves bye-bye, plays pat-a-cake, points
18 monthsWalks unsupported, runs (falls), climbs stairs with helpStacks 2–3 blocks, scribbles6–20 words, points to body partsSymbolic play, feeds self with spoon
2 yearsRuns well, kicks ball, walks up/down stairsStacks 6 blocks, turns pages, copies vertical line2-word phrases ★, 50+ words, 50% intelligibleParallel play, says "no", possessive
3 yearsRides tricycle, stands on 1 foot 3 secStacks 9 blocks, copies circle, uses scissors3-word sentences, 75% intelligible, knows coloursGroup play, imaginative play, toilet trained
4 yearsHops, skips, stairs alternating feetCopies cross/square, draws person (4 parts)Full sentences, tells stories, 100% intelligibleCooperative play, counts to 10, knows address
5 yearsSkips, catches ball reliably, rides bicycle (with training wheels)Copies triangle, writes nameFluent speech, follows complex instructionsSchool readiness, understands rules, reads letters
🟡 Developmental red flags — refer immediately
No social smile by 3 months · No babble by 12 months · No words by 16 months · No 2-word phrases by 24 months · ANY loss of previously acquired language or social skills at ANY age (regression = refer urgently, possible ASD/Rett). Also refer for: motor asymmetry at any age, persistent fisting after 3 months, no sitting by 9 months.

cards.title: Paediatric Normal Ranges — Quick Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
ParameterNeonate (0–1m)Infant (1–12m)Toddler (1–3y)Child (3–12y)Adolescent (12–18y)
HR (bpm)120–160100–16090–15070–12060–100
RR (breaths/min)40–6030–5024–4018–3012–20
SBP (mmHg)60–9080–10085–10090–110100–130
Temp (°C)36.5–37.536.5–37.536.5–37.536.5–37.536.5–37.5
Weight estimation (Paediatric emergency)
APLS formula: Weight (kg) = (age + 4) × 2 [1–10 years]
Neonate: Birth weight ≈ 3.5kg, regain by 2 weeks. Double by 5m. Triple by 1 year.
WETFLAG (resus): Weight · Energy (4J/kg defibrillation) · Tube (age/4+4 mm uncuffed ETT) · Fluids (10ml/kg bolus) · Lorazepam (0.1mg/kg) · Adrenaline (0.01mg/kg) · Glucose (2ml/kg 10% dextrose)
Lab valueNeonatalInfant–ChildNotes
Hb (g/dL)14–22 (physiological anaemia nadir at 8–12 wk: ~9–11)11–14 (child)Iron-rich weaning foods from 6 months
WBC (×10⁹/L)9–306–17Neutrophilia at birth; lymphocyte predominance at 1 week
Glucose (mmol/L)2.5–6.03.5–6.0Hypoglycaemia (<2.6) = treat urgently in any child
Bilirubin (µmol/L) jaundiceTreat if >level-chart. ≥340 µmol/L at any age = kernicterus riskN/APhysiological jaundice peaks day 3–4, clears by 2 weeks

cards.title: Childhood Vaccination Schedule — India (IAP 2023)
cards._legacyHtml:
AgeVaccines
BirthBCG + OPV-0 + HBV-1 (within 24 hours)
6 weeksDTwP/DTaP-1 + IPV-1 + HBV-2 + Hib-1 + Rotavirus-1 + PCV-1
10 weeksDTwP/DTaP-2 + IPV-2 + Hib-2 + Rotavirus-2 + PCV-2
14 weeksDTwP/DTaP-3 + IPV-3 + Hib-3 + Rotavirus-3 + PCV-3
6 monthsHBV-3 + OPV-1 + (Influenza-1 if opted)
9 monthsOPV-2 + MMR-1 (Measles, Mumps, Rubella)
12 monthsHepatitis A-1 + Varicella-1 + PCV booster
15 monthsMMR-2 + Varicella-2 + DTwP/DTaP booster-1 + Hib booster + IPV booster
18 monthsHepatitis A-2
2 yearsTyphoid conjugate vaccine (TCV)
4–6 yearsDTwP/DTaP booster-2 + OPV-3 + MMR-3
10–12 yearsTdap + HPV (2 doses, girls/boys) + TCV booster
🟡 Vaccine catch-up
Child presenting without vaccination records: restart series using minimum intervals. OPV + MMR can be given simultaneously in different limbs. BCG has no upper age limit if not previously given in HIV-negative children. Always document lot number, site, route. Observe 15–30 min post-vaccination for immediate reactions. Paracetamol prophylaxis reduces pain/fever post-vaccination (15mg/kg every 4–6 hours PRN).

cards.title: Paediatric Emergencies — Febrile Child
cards._legacyHtml:
Traffic light system — NICE CG160 (modified)
Assess fever ≥38°C (rectal) in all children under 5
Feature🟢 Low Risk🟡 Intermediate Risk🔴 High Risk — Act NOW
ColourNormal skin/lips/tonguePallor reported by parentPale/mottled/ashen/blue
ActivityNormal, smiles, stays awakeNot smiling, decreased activityNo response, cannot be roused, weak cry
BreathingNormalNasal flaring, tachypnoeaGrunting, severe recession, O2 sat <92%
Circulation/hydrationMoist mucous membranes, normal skinDry mucous membranesReduced skin turgor, mottled, cap refill >3s
OtherNo red featuresNo focus of infection, age 3–6m with T≥39°C, fever >5 daysNon-blanching rash ★, neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, seizure, age <3m T≥38°C
Non-blanching rash = meningococcal until proven otherwise
Glass test: rash that does NOT fade under glass pressure = petechiae/purpura → septicaemia. DO NOT wait. IV/IM ceftriaxone immediately + 999/108 transfer.
🟡 Febrile convulsions
Peak age 6 months–5 years. Simple febrile convulsion: generalised, <15 min, single in 24hr, full recovery within 1hr. Management: airway + recovery position + time it. IV lorazepam 0.1mg/kg if >5 min. Reassure parents: risk of epilepsy only slightly above background. Recurrence risk ~30%. Investigate for source of fever. LP only if meningism, petechiae, or complex features.

cards.title: Childhood Nutrition — Key Nutrients & Deficiencies
cards._legacyHtml:
NutrientAge-Specific NeedDeficiency SignsSources & Supplementation
Iron ★6–24 months: highest risk. Premature infants: supplement from 2 weeksPallor, irritability, poor weight gain, developmental delay, ADHD-like symptomsFortified cereals, pureed meat, legumes. Iron drops if breastfed beyond 6 months without iron-rich solids
Vitamin DAll ages: 400IU (0–1yr), 600IU (1–18yr). Higher in deficiencyRickets (bowing legs, delayed fontanelle closure), poor immunity, dental problemsSunlight + D3 drops for infants. Supplements essential in dark-skinned, low-sun regions
Omega-3 (DHA) ★Critical 0–2yrs for brain development. Continue throughout childhoodPoor attention, learning difficulties, dry skin, behavioural issuesBreast milk (if mother sufficient), formula (DHA-fortified), oily fish from 6 months, algae oil supplements
ZincCritical for growth, immunity, cognitive developmentGrowth faltering, recurrent infections, poor appetite, delayed wound healingMeat, legumes, pumpkin seeds. Supplement 5–10mg/day if deficient
Calcium1–3yrs: 700mg/day. 4–8yrs: 1000mg/day. 9–18yrs: 1300mg/dayPoor bone density, dental problems, muscle crampsDairy or fortified plant milks, leafy greens, sardines. Avoid excess — inhibits iron absorption
IodineEssential for thyroid function and brain developmentGoitre, hypothyroidism, cognitive impairment — most preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwideIodised salt, seafood, dairy. Supplement in pregnancy and breastfeeding
🟡 Introduction of Solids — Current Evidence
Introduce solids at 6 months (not before 4 months). Current evidence SUPPORTS early introduction of allergenic foods (peanut, egg, fish) at 6 months — this REDUCES allergy risk (LEAP trial). Baby-led weaning (BLW) is safe and improves food acceptance, provided iron-rich finger foods are offered. Avoid: honey (botulism <1yr), whole nuts (choking <5yrs), added sugar and salt (<1yr), low-fat dairy (<2yrs). Vitamin D drops for all breastfed infants from birth — breast milk is deficient in Vit D regardless of maternal status.

cards.title: Naturopathy for Children — Safe Practice Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Paediatric Naturopathy — Key Principles
Children are NOT small adults. Doses, safety profiles, and developmental considerations differ significantly. Always calculate weight-based doses. Some adult herbs and supplements are contraindicated in children.
ModalitySafe FromKey ModificationsContraindications in Children
HydrotherapyBirthTemperature must be tepid (34–36°C for infants). Cold applications — brief only (30 sec). No contrast bath <2yrsFever >39°C (tepid sponging only, no cold immersion)
MassageBirthInfant massage: gentle effleurage. Coconut or sesame oil. 10–15 min. Improves weight gain in premature infants (RCT evidence)Skin infections, active dermatitis at site
AcupressureBirthGentle fingertip pressure only. No needles <7yrs unless specialist paediatric acupuncturist. Key points: PC6 (nausea), ST36 (immunity), SP6 (sleep)N/A for acupressure
Herbal medicineVaries — see belowDoses 1/6 adult (infant), 1/4 (toddler), 1/2 (school-age). Use standardised preparationsSee contraindicated herbs below
Yoga/movement2 yearsPlay-based, unstructured. Structured yoga from 4–5yrs. Focus on fun and body awareness, not performanceN/A
Nutrition therapyBirthAge-appropriate modifications. Family-centred — whole family diet change more effective than targeting child aloneRestrictive diets need dietitian involvement
HerbSafe in ChildrenCommon UseAvoid
Chamomile✅ All ages (tea)Colic, sleep, anxiety, GI upsetRagweed allergy
Echinacea✅ Over 2yrsRecurrent URTIs, immune supportAutoimmune disease
Ginger✅ Over 2yrsNausea, motion sickness, colicBleeding disorders
Valerian✅ Over 3yrsInsomnia, anxietyLiver disease
St John's Wort⚠️ Use with caution >12yrsMild-moderate depressionPhotosensitising medications, bipolar
Licorice root❌ Avoid long-term in children–HTN risk, hormonal effects
Kava❌ Contraindicated <18yrs–Hepatotoxicity

cards.title: Childhood Obesity & Metabolic Health
cards._legacyHtml:
Childhood Obesity — Global Crisis
1 in 3 children in India is overweight or obese (ICMR 2023). Childhood obesity tracks to adult obesity in 80% of cases. Metabolic complications (T2DM, fatty liver, dyslipidaemia, PCOS) now appear in adolescence. Prevention and early intervention are critical.
AssessmentMethodThresholds
BMIWeight(kg)/height(m)²Overweight: >85th centile. Obese: >95th centile (use age/sex-specific charts)
Waist circumferenceAt umbilicus, end of expiration>90th centile for age = abdominal obesity (metabolic risk)
Blood pressureAppropriate cuff sizeUse age/sex/height-specific tables — not adult thresholds
Fasting glucose + insulin8-hr fastHOMA-IR >3.5 in children = insulin resistance
Liver (NAFLD screening)ALT, ultrasound abdomenALT >2x ULN in obese child = NAFLD until proven otherwise
1
Family-Centred Approach ★ Targeting the child alone is ineffective. The whole family must change. Remove junk food from home (environment design). Cook together. Eat together (family meals reduce childhood obesity 40%). Screen time <2hrs/day. Outdoor play 60 min/day minimum.
2
Dietary Modification Eliminate: sugary drinks (single most impactful change), ultra-processed snacks, fast food. Add: vegetables at every meal, whole fruits (not juice), whole grains, protein at breakfast (reduces appetite 20% through the day). Portion sizes: use child's hand — palm = protein, fist = carb, thumb = fat.
3
Physical Activity 60 min moderate-vigorous activity daily (WHO). Reduce sedentary screen time. Sports participation improves self-esteem — crucial for avoiding emotional eating. Avoid weight-focused messaging — focus on fitness, strength, and fun. Never shame a child about weight.
🟡 Never Put a Child on a Calorie-Restricted Diet
Calorie restriction in growing children risks nutrient deficiency, growth stunting, disordered eating, and psychological harm. Goal is weight MAINTENANCE while child grows into their weight — not weight LOSS (except in severe cases under specialist supervision). Focus on: food quality over quantity, movement as fun not punishment, body positivity and self-respect. Children who are shamed about their weight have higher rates of eating disorders, depression, and adult obesity.

cards.title: Common Childhood Illnesses — Naturopathic
cards._legacyHtml:
Childhood Illness — When to Treat and When to Refer
Most childhood illnesses are viral, self-limiting, and do not require antibiotics. Naturopathic management reduces antibiotic overuse and supports healthy immune development. However, knowing RED FLAGS that require immediate medical attention is critical — this is non-negotiable for patient safety.
ConditionFirst-Line NaturopathicEvidenceRed Flags → Refer
Viral URTI (common cold)Zinc lozenges 13–23mg (ionic zinc — dissolve in mouth) ★★★ started within 24hrs — reduces duration 33%. Elderberry (Sambucus nigra) 15ml syrup 4×/day ★★★ — reduces duration 4 days. Honey 1 tsp ★★★ (WHO recommends honey for cough in children >1 year — comparable to dextromethorphan). Saline nasal irrigation ★★★. Vit D 1000 IU/day prevention.★★★ All above Cochrane-supportedFebrile seizure, neck stiffness (meningism), severe dehydration, rash with fever, earache that doesn't settle in 72hrs
Acute Otitis Media (ear infection)65% resolve without antibiotics in 3 days. Pain: warm oil drops (naturopathic), paracetamol/ibuprofen (conventional). Zinc 10mg/day prevention ★★★. Probiotics (reduce incidence: L. rhamnosus GG ★★). Xylitol chewing gum ★★ (inhibits Streptococcus pneumoniae).★★★ Cochrane: no antibiotic superiority vs placebo in uncomplicated AOM in age >2Under 6 months: always refer. Bilateral AOM. Perforation. Fever >39°C. Symptoms >72hrs no improvement. Mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling/erythema).
TonsillitisViral (80% of tonsillitis): supportive — warm salt water gargle, honey, popsicles, adequate hydration, paracetamol. Manuka honey ★★. Echinacea syrup ★★ (children's formulation). Viral: NO antibiotics.★★ Echinacea reduces severity in paediatric URTIExudate + fever + tender nodes + no cough (Centor criteria — likely strep): URGENT throat swab ± antibiotics (Group A strep). Trismus or drooling: peritonsillar abscess — EMERGENCY.
Fever ManagementFever <38.5°C: no need to treat (fever is adaptive — raises temperature above bacterial optimum). Keep child comfortable, hydrated. Tepid sponging ★ (controversial — limited evidence). Paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever >38.5°C AND distress.Evidence-based approachInfant <3 months + ANY fever: REFER immediately. Febrile seizure: refer for assessment (99% benign but workup needed). Fever >5 days: investigate. Meningism: EMERGENCY (bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, non-blanching rash, photophobia).
Infant ColicLactobacillus reuteri DSM 17938 drops ★★★ most evidence-based intervention — reduces crying 50% in breastfed infants (Cochrane 2018). Simethicone: no better than placebo. Probiotics (L. reuteri) safe from birth.★★★ L. reuteri — Cochrane Grade AVomiting (projectile = pyloric stenosis), blood in stool, weight loss, fever — all require medical investigation to exclude non-colic causes.

cards.title: Childhood Eczema & Allergies
cards._legacyHtml:
The Atopic March — Early Intervention Opportunity
Atopic eczema (AE) is the first manifestation of the atopic march — appearing in 60% of cases by 6 months of age. The gut microbiome in the first 1000 days of life (conception to age 2) programs immune response and allergy risk. This window is the most important preventive opportunity in paediatric allergy medicine.
StrategyEvidenceProtocol
Emollient therapy from birth★★★ BEEP trial: daily emollient from 2 weeks reduces eczema incidence 50% in high-risk infantsApply fragrance-free emollient (white soft paraffin, emulsifying ointment, or ceramide-based moisturiser) twice daily from first weeks of life in high-risk infants (family history of atopy). "Slather and smother" principle — thick application, not sparingly. Cheaper emollients as effective as expensive brands.
Probiotics in pregnancy + infant★★★ Cochrane 2022: L. rhamnosus GG during pregnancy + infant reduces eczema risk 22%Mother: L. rhamnosus GG 10⁹ CFU from 36 weeks → throughout breastfeeding. Infant: LGG drops from birth to 6 months. Note: evidence strongest for prevention, less clear for treatment of established eczema.
Early allergen introduction (LEAP/EAT)★★★ LEAP trial: peanut protein from 4–11 months reduces peanut allergy 81% in high-risk infantsHigh-risk infants (severe eczema or egg allergy): refer to paediatric allergist for guided introduction. For all other infants: introduce common allergens (peanut, egg, wheat, fish, soy, sesame) by 6 months — do not delay. "Wig Bag" approach: introduce all 8 major allergens early and regularly.
Dietary management of established eczema★★ Food sensitivity triggers eczema in 30–40% of children under 5 with moderate-severe eczemaElimination + challenge: IgE-mediated food allergy (cow's milk, egg most common — skin prick test or sIgE). Non-IgE food sensitivity: elimination trial 6 weeks then reintroduction. Caution: unnecessary restriction diets → nutritional deficiency and food anxiety. Always with dietitian supervision.
1
Naturopathic Eczema Protocol Omega-3 1g/day (as fish oil from 6 months of solid food) ★★. Evening primrose oil 500mg–1g (gamma-linolenic acid — anti-inflammatory, skin barrier) ★★. Vit D 1000 IU ★★ (deficiency common in eczema patients — VDR regulates antimicrobial peptides + skin barrier function). Zinc 5–10mg (supports skin barrier, wound healing). Probiotics ★★ (mixed evidence for treatment vs prevention — try L. rhamnosus GG + Bifidobacterium). Topical naturopathic: sunflower seed oil ★★★ (preserves skin barrier — cheap, accessible). Coconut oil ★★ (antimicrobial, moisturising — good for Staphylococcus aureus colonisation in eczema). Avoid: olive oil topically (oleic acid disrupts ceramide barrier).

cards.title: Adolescent Health — Naturopathic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
Adolescence — The Second Critical Window of Brain Development
Adolescence (10–24 years per WHO definition) is characterised by dramatic brain remodelling (prefrontal cortex last to mature — until age 25), hormonal surges, identity formation, peer influence, and high-risk behaviour. It is also a critical window for establishing healthy habits that persist for life. Naturopathic investment in adolescent health has outsized lifelong returns.
AreaNaturopathic AssessmentKey Interventions
Mental health screeningPHQ-A (adolescent version), SCARED (anxiety), AUDIT (alcohol), CRAFFT (substance use). Screen at every well-visit from age 11.Omega-3 ★★★ (EPA 1g/day for depression — strong evidence in adolescent depression). Exercise ★★★ (most effective antidepressant in adolescents without side effects). Sleep 8–10 hrs ★★★. Limit screen time <2hrs recreational. MBSR for anxiety ★★★.
Acne protocolAssess: dietary triggers (high-GI diet, dairy ★★), stress, OCP (for females), hormonal pattern (testosterone, DHEAS, insulin).Low-GI diet ★★★ (RCT: reduces inflammatory acne 50%). Zinc picolinate 30mg ★★★ (comparable to tetracycline in RCTs for inflammatory acne). Omega-3 reduces inflammatory acne ★★. Topical: tea tree oil 5% ★★★ (comparable to 5% benzoyl peroxide). Probiotics ★★ (gut-skin axis). Avoid: skim milk (more IGF-1 than full fat), high-GI foods, whey protein (acnegenic).
Sports nutritionAdolescent athletes: higher protein needs (1.4–1.7g/kg), ↑ calcium (bone mass accrual peak), ↑ iron (adolescent girls). Screen for: RED-S (relative energy deficiency).Protein: 1.4–1.7g/kg. Calcium 1300mg/day (adolescent peak bone mass). Iron 18mg (girls with heavy periods + sport). Magnesium (sleep + muscle function + cramping). Creatine: generally safe from age 18 (limited data under 18). Avoid: unregulated supplements, stimulants, protein bars with undisclosed additives.
SleepNatural circadian delay in puberty — biologically normal to not feel tired until midnight. Social jetlag is epidemic in adolescents.Education (NOT lectures) about sleep importance. Melatonin 0.5mg (phase advance — given 5–6hrs before desired sleep time). Blue-light glasses from 8pm. Consistent weekend wake time (within 1 hour of weekday). Later school start time advocacy.
Digital wellnessAverage Indian adolescent: 6–8 hours daily screen time. Displaced: sleep, exercise, in-person social interaction, creativity.Tech boundaries: no phone in bedroom, phone off at 9pm. Monotasking (one screen at a time). "Dopamine fast" — 24hr break from screens weekly. Offline hobbies prescription. Parental modelling is key.

cards.title: Neonatal Care — BNYS Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
Neonatal Care — BNYS Knowledge Domain
BNYS graduates may encounter newborns in community settings, mother-infant clinics, and home visits. Basic neonatal assessment and common conditions knowledge enables safe practice — and appropriate escalation when needed.
TopicKey Clinical Points
Neonatal Jaundice (Neonatal Hyperbilirubinaemia)Physiological jaundice: appears day 2–3, peaks day 4–5, resolves by day 10 (term). Pathological: appears day 1 (suspect haemolytic — blood group incompatibility), direct hyperbilirubinaemia (pale stools, dark urine = biliary atresia), jaundice persisting >14 days (term) or >21 days (preterm). Management: phototherapy threshold — use Bhutani nomogram by age in hours. Breastfeeding jaundice: ensure adequate milk transfer (not starvation jaundice). Naturopathic: sunlight exposure (indirect — not direct midday sun) may help mild physiological jaundice via skin photoisomerisation of bilirubin. Evidence weak — never delay phototherapy for sunlight therapy.
Newborn Reflexes — Normal AssessmentMoro (startle): arms extend then flex — birth to 4 months. Absent = brachial plexus injury, cerebral depression. Rooting + sucking: birth → 4 months. Critical for breastfeeding. Palmar grasp: birth → 5–6 months. Plantar grasp: birth → 9–12 months. Stepping: birth → 2 months. Asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR): fencing position — persists to 4 months. Persistent reflexes beyond expected age = neurological concern → refer developmental paediatrician.
Infant Colic ManagementRome IV: crying >3 hrs/day, >3 days/week, >3 weeks in healthy infant under 3 months. Peaks at 6 weeks. Resolves by 3–4 months. Evidence-based: L. reuteri DSM 17938 drops ★★★ (Cochrane: significantly reduces crying in breastfed infants). Maternal elimination diet (dairy, caffeine) for breastfed infants if suspected cow's milk protein allergy. Rocking, white noise, gentle abdominal massage (clockwise). Simethicone: no better than placebo. Gripe water: no evidence. Important: screen for maternal anxiety and depression — colic is a significant cause of postpartum PND and child abuse risk.
Newborn Physical Examination ChecklistHead: fontanelle tension (bulging = ↑ICP, sunken = dehydration). Eyes: red reflex bilaterally (absent = cataract/retinoblastoma → URGENT). Palate: cleft. Heart: murmur (benign vs pathological — refer cardiology if >grade 3 or associated features). Abdomen: organomegaly, hernias. Genitalia: ambiguous genitalia → endocrinology urgently. Hips: Barlow and Ortolani for DDH. Spine: sacral dimple (>5mm from anus or hairy pit → USS spine). Tone: hypo or hypertonia. Skin: birth marks (café au lait >6 = NF1 risk).
🟡 Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) — The Most Powerful Neonatal Intervention
KMC (skin-to-skin contact between mother and newborn) has the most evidence of any neonatal intervention: ★★★ Cochrane: reduces preterm infant mortality 40%, reduces hypothermia, stabilises heart rate and SpO2, reduces infection risk, improves breastfeeding, improves neurodevelopmental outcomes, reduces maternal PND. Protocol: bare infant prone on mother's chest (or father's) continuously for >8 hours/day. Works in term infants too — reduces cortisol, improves attachment, extends breastfeeding. Promote KMC in ALL BNYS maternal health work — it is free, requires no equipment, and saves lives.

cards._legacyHtml:
NutrientAge-Specific NeedDeficiency SignsSources & Supplementation
Iron ★6–24 months: highest risk. Premature infants: supplement from 2 weeksPallor, irritability, poor weight gain, developmental delay, ADHD-like symptomsFortified cereals, pureed meat, legumes. Iron drops if breastfed beyond 6 months without iron-rich solids
Vitamin DAll ages: 400IU (0–1yr), 600IU (1–18yr). Higher in deficiencyRickets (bowing legs, delayed fontanelle closure), poor immunity, dental problemsSunlight + D3 drops for infants. Supplements essential in dark-skinned, low-sun regions

ZincCritical for growth, immunity, cognitive developmentGrowth faltering, recurrent infections, poor appetite, delayed wound healingMeat, legumes, pumpkin seeds. Supplement 5–10mg/day if deficient
Calcium1–3yrs: 700mg/day. 4–8yrs: 1000mg/day. 9–18yrs: 1300mg/dayPoor bone density, dental problems, muscle crampsDairy or fortified plant milks, leafy greens, sardines. Avoid excess — inhibits iron absorption
IodineEssential for thyroid function and brain developmentGoitre, hypothyroidism, cognitive impairment — most preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwideIodised salt, seafood, dairy. Supplement in pregnancy and breastfeeding
🟡 Introduction of Solids — Current Evidence
Introduce solids at 6 months (not before 4 months). Current evidence SUPPORTS early introduction of allergenic foods (peanut, egg, fish) at 6 months — this REDUCES allergy risk (LEAP trial). Baby-led weaning (BLW) is safe and improves food acceptance, provided iron-rich finger foods are offered. Avoid: honey (botulism <1yr), whole nuts (choking <5yrs), added sugar and salt (<1yr), low-fat dairy (<2yrs). Vitamin D drops for all breastfed infants from birth — breast milk is deficient in Vit D regardless of maternal status.

cards._legacyHtml:
Paediatric Naturopathy — Key Principles
Children are NOT small adults. Doses, safety profiles, and developmental considerations differ significantly. Always calculate weight-based doses. Some adult herbs and supplements are contraindicated in children.
ModalitySafe FromKey ModificationsContraindications in Children
HydrotherapyBirthTemperature must be tepid (34–36°C for infants). Cold applications — brief only (30 sec). No contrast bath <2yrsFever >39°C (tepid sponging only, no cold immersion)

Herbal medicineVaries — see belowDoses 1/6 adult (infant), 1/4 (toddler), 1/2 (school-age). Use standardised preparationsSee contraindicated herbs below
Yoga/movement2 yearsPlay-based, unstructured. Structured yoga from 4–5yrs. Focus on fun and body awareness, not performanceN/A
Nutrition therapyBirthAge-appropriate modifications. Family-centred — whole family diet change more effective than targeting child aloneRestrictive diets need dietitian involvement

Chamomile✅ All ages (tea)Colic, sleep, anxiety, GI upsetRagweed allergy

Kava❌ Contraindicated <18yrs–Hepatotoxicity

cards.title: Childhood Obesity & Metabolic Health
cards._legacyHtml:
Childhood Obesity — Global Crisis
1 in 3 children in India is overweight or obese (ICMR 2023). Childhood obesity tracks to adult obesity in 80% of cases. Metabolic complications (T2DM, fatty liver, dyslipidaemia, PCOS) now appear in adolescence. Prevention and early intervention are critical.
AssessmentMethodThresholds
BMIWeight(kg)/height(m)²Overweight: >85th centile. Obese: >95th centile (use age/sex-specific charts)
Waist circumferenceAt umbilicus, end of expiration>90th centile for age = abdominal obesity (metabolic risk)
Blood pressureAppropriate cuff sizeUse age/sex/height-specific tables — not adult thresholds
Fasting glucose + insulin8-hr fastHOMA-IR >3.5 in children = insulin resistance
Liver (NAFLD screening)ALT, ultrasound abdomenALT >2x ULN in obese child = NAFLD until proven otherwise
1
Family-Centred Approach ★ Targeting the child alone is ineffective. The whole family must change. Remove junk food from home (environment design). Cook together. Eat together (family meals reduce childhood obesity 40%). Screen time <2hrs/day. Outdoor play 60 min/day minimum.
2
Dietary Modification Eliminate: sugary drinks (single most impactful change), ultra-processed snacks, fast food. Add: vegetables at every meal, whole fruits (not juice), whole grains, protein at breakfast (reduces appetite 20% through the day). Portion sizes: use child's hand — palm = protein, fist = carb, thumb = fat.
3
Physical Activity 60 min moderate-vigorous activity daily (WHO). Reduce sedentary screen time. Sports participation improves self-esteem — crucial for avoiding emotional eating. Avoid weight-focused messaging — focus on fitness, strength, and fun. Never shame a child about weight.
🟡 Never Put a Child on a Calorie-Restricted Diet
Calorie restriction in growing children risks nutrient deficiency, growth stunting, disordered eating, and psychological harm. Goal is weight MAINTENANCE while child grows into their weight — not weight LOSS (except in severe cases under specialist supervision). Focus on: food quality over quantity, movement as fun not punishment, body positivity and self-respect. Children who are shamed about their weight have higher rates of eating disorders, depression, and adult obesity.

cards.title: Neonatal Care — BNYS Reference
cards._legacyHtml:
Neonatal Care — BNYS Knowledge Domain
BNYS graduates may encounter newborns in community settings, mother-infant clinics, and home visits. Basic neonatal assessment and common conditions knowledge enables safe practice — and appropriate escalation when needed.
TopicKey Clinical Points
Neonatal Jaundice (Neonatal Hyperbilirubinaemia)Physiological jaundice: appears day 2–3, peaks day 4–5, resolves by day 10 (term). Pathological: appears day 1 (suspect haemolytic — blood group incompatibility), direct hyperbilirubinaemia (pale stools, dark urine = biliary atresia), jaundice persisting >14 days (term) or >21 days (preterm). Management: phototherapy threshold — use Bhutani nomogram by age in hours. Breastfeeding jaundice: ensure adequate milk transfer (not starvation jaundice). Naturopathic: sunlight exposure (indirect — not direct midday sun) may help mild physiological jaundice via skin photoisomerisation of bilirubin. Evidence weak — never delay phototherapy for sunlight therapy.
Newborn Reflexes — Normal AssessmentMoro (startle): arms extend then flex — birth to 4 months. Absent = brachial plexus injury, cerebral depression. Rooting + sucking: birth → 4 months. Critical for breastfeeding. Palmar grasp: birth → 5–6 months. Plantar grasp: birth → 9–12 months. Stepping: birth → 2 months. Asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR): fencing position — persists to 4 months. Persistent reflexes beyond expected age = neurological concern → refer developmental paediatrician.
Infant Colic ManagementRome IV: crying >3 hrs/day, >3 days/week, >3 weeks in healthy infant under 3 months. Peaks at 6 weeks. Resolves by 3–4 months. Evidence-based: L. reuteri DSM 17938 drops ★★★ (Cochrane: significantly reduces crying in breastfed infants). Maternal elimination diet (dairy, caffeine) for breastfed infants if suspected cow's milk protein allergy. Rocking, white noise, gentle abdominal massage (clockwise). Simethicone: no better than placebo. Gripe water: no evidence. Important: screen for maternal anxiety and depression — colic is a significant cause of postpartum PND and child abuse risk.
Newborn Physical Examination ChecklistHead: fontanelle tension (bulging = ↑ICP, sunken = dehydration). Eyes: red reflex bilaterally (absent = cataract/retinoblastoma → URGENT). Palate: cleft. Heart: murmur (benign vs pathological — refer cardiology if >grade 3 or associated features). Abdomen: organomegaly, hernias. Genitalia: ambiguous genitalia → endocrinology urgently. Hips: Barlow and Ortolani for DDH. Spine: sacral dimple (>5mm from anus or hairy pit → USS spine). Tone: hypo or hypertonia. Skin: birth marks (café au lait >6 = NF1 risk).
🟡 Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) — The Most Powerful Neonatal Intervention
KMC (skin-to-skin contact between mother and newborn) has the most evidence of any neonatal intervention: ★★★ Cochrane: reduces preterm infant mortality 40%, reduces hypothermia, stabilises heart rate and SpO2, reduces infection risk, improves breastfeeding, improves neurodevelopmental outcomes, reduces maternal PND. Protocol: bare infant prone on mother's chest (or father's) continuously for >8 hours/day. Works in term infants too — reduces cortisol, improves attachment, extends breastfeeding. Promote KMC in ALL BNYS maternal health work — it is free, requires no equipment, and saves lives.

cards.title: Common Childhood Illnesses — Naturopathic
cards._legacyHtml:
Childhood Illness — When to Treat and When to Refer
Most childhood illnesses are viral, self-limiting, and do not require antibiotics. Naturopathic management reduces antibiotic overuse and supports healthy immune development. However, knowing RED FLAGS that require immediate medical attention is critical — this is non-negotiable for patient safety.
ConditionFirst-Line NaturopathicEvidenceRed Flags → Refer
Viral URTI (common cold)Zinc lozenges 13–23mg (ionic zinc — dissolve in mouth) ★★★ started within 24hrs — reduces duration 33%. Elderberry (Sambucus nigra) 15ml syrup 4×/day ★★★ — reduces duration 4 days. Honey 1 tsp ★★★ (WHO recommends honey for cough in children >1 year — comparable to dextromethorphan). Saline nasal irrigation ★★★. Vit D 1000 IU/day prevention.★★★ All above Cochrane-supportedFebrile seizure, neck stiffness (meningism), severe dehydration, rash with fever, earache that doesn't settle in 72hrs
Acute Otitis Media (ear infection)65% resolve without antibiotics in 3 days. Pain: warm oil drops (naturopathic), paracetamol/ibuprofen (conventional). Zinc 10mg/day prevention ★★★. Probiotics (reduce incidence: L. rhamnosus GG ★★). Xylitol chewing gum ★★ (inhibits Streptococcus pneumoniae).★★★ Cochrane: no antibiotic superiority vs placebo in uncomplicated AOM in age >2Under 6 months: always refer. Bilateral AOM. Perforation. Fever >39°C. Symptoms >72hrs no improvement. Mastoiditis (post-auricular swelling/erythema).
TonsillitisViral (80% of tonsillitis): supportive — warm salt water gargle, honey, popsicles, adequate hydration, paracetamol. Manuka honey ★★. Echinacea syrup ★★ (children's formulation). Viral: NO antibiotics.★★ Echinacea reduces severity in paediatric URTIExudate + fever + tender nodes + no cough (Centor criteria — likely strep): URGENT throat swab ± antibiotics (Group A strep). Trismus or drooling: peritonsillar abscess — EMERGENCY.
Fever ManagementFever <38.5°C: no need to treat (fever is adaptive — raises temperature above bacterial optimum). Keep child comfortable, hydrated. Tepid sponging ★ (controversial — limited evidence). Paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever >38.5°C AND distress.Evidence-based approachInfant <3 months + ANY fever: REFER immediately. Febrile seizure: refer for assessment (99% benign but workup needed). Fever >5 days: investigate. Meningism: EMERGENCY (bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, non-blanching rash, photophobia).
Infant ColicLactobacillus reuteri DSM 17938 drops ★★★ most evidence-based intervention — reduces crying 50% in breastfed infants (Cochrane 2018). Simethicone: no better than placebo. Probiotics (L. reuteri) safe from birth.★★★ L. reuteri — Cochrane Grade AVomiting (projectile = pyloric stenosis), blood in stool, weight loss, fever — all require medical investigation to exclude non-colic causes.

cards.title: Childhood Eczema & Allergies
cards._legacyHtml:
The Atopic March — Early Intervention Opportunity
Atopic eczema (AE) is the first manifestation of the atopic march — appearing in 60% of cases by 6 months of age. The gut microbiome in the first 1000 days of life (conception to age 2) programs immune response and allergy risk. This window is the most important preventive opportunity in paediatric allergy medicine.
StrategyEvidenceProtocol
Emollient therapy from birth★★★ BEEP trial: daily emollient from 2 weeks reduces eczema incidence 50% in high-risk infantsApply fragrance-free emollient (white soft paraffin, emulsifying ointment, or ceramide-based moisturiser) twice daily from first weeks of life in high-risk infants (family history of atopy). "Slather and smother" principle — thick application, not sparingly. Cheaper emollients as effective as expensive brands.
Probiotics in pregnancy + infant★★★ Cochrane 2022: L. rhamnosus GG during pregnancy + infant reduces eczema risk 22%Mother: L. rhamnosus GG 10⁹ CFU from 36 weeks → throughout breastfeeding. Infant: LGG drops from birth to 6 months. Note: evidence strongest for prevention, less clear for treatment of established eczema.
Early allergen introduction (LEAP/EAT)★★★ LEAP trial: peanut protein from 4–11 months reduces peanut allergy 81% in high-risk infantsHigh-risk infants (severe eczema or egg allergy): refer to paediatric allergist for guided introduction. For all other infants: introduce common allergens (peanut, egg, wheat, fish, soy, sesame) by 6 months — do not delay. "Wig Bag" approach: introduce all 8 major allergens early and regularly.
Dietary management of established eczema★★ Food sensitivity triggers eczema in 30–40% of children under 5 with moderate-severe eczemaElimination + challenge: IgE-mediated food allergy (cow's milk, egg most common — skin prick test or sIgE). Non-IgE food sensitivity: elimination trial 6 weeks then reintroduction. Caution: unnecessary restriction diets → nutritional deficiency and food anxiety. Always with dietitian supervision.
1
Naturopathic Eczema Protocol Omega-3 1g/day (as fish oil from 6 months of solid food) ★★. Evening primrose oil 500mg–1g (gamma-linolenic acid — anti-inflammatory, skin barrier) ★★. Vit D 1000 IU ★★ (deficiency common in eczema patients — VDR regulates antimicrobial peptides + skin barrier function). Zinc 5–10mg (supports skin barrier, wound healing). Probiotics ★★ (mixed evidence for treatment vs prevention — try L. rhamnosus GG + Bifidobacterium). Topical naturopathic: sunflower seed oil ★★★ (preserves skin barrier — cheap, accessible). Coconut oil ★★ (antimicrobial, moisturising — good for Staphylococcus aureus colonisation in eczema). Avoid: olive oil topically (oleic acid disrupts ceramide barrier).

cards.title: Adolescent Health — Naturopathic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
Adolescence — The Second Critical Window of Brain Development
Adolescence (10–24 years per WHO definition) is characterised by dramatic brain remodelling (prefrontal cortex last to mature — until age 25), hormonal surges, identity formation, peer influence, and high-risk behaviour. It is also a critical window for establishing healthy habits that persist for life. Naturopathic investment in adolescent health has outsized lifelong returns.
AreaNaturopathic AssessmentKey Interventions
Mental health screeningPHQ-A (adolescent version), SCARED (anxiety), AUDIT (alcohol), CRAFFT (substance use). Screen at every well-visit from age 11.Omega-3 ★★★ (EPA 1g/day for depression — strong evidence in adolescent depression). Exercise ★★★ (most effective antidepressant in adolescents without side effects). Sleep 8–10 hrs ★★★. Limit screen time <2hrs recreational. MBSR for anxiety ★★★.
Acne protocolAssess: dietary triggers (high-GI diet, dairy ★★), stress, OCP (for females), hormonal pattern (testosterone, DHEAS, insulin).Low-GI diet ★★★ (RCT: reduces inflammatory acne 50%). Zinc picolinate 30mg ★★★ (comparable to tetracycline in RCTs for inflammatory acne). Omega-3 reduces inflammatory acne ★★. Topical: tea tree oil 5% ★★★ (comparable to 5% benzoyl peroxide). Probiotics ★★ (gut-skin axis). Avoid: skim milk (more IGF-1 than full fat), high-GI foods, whey protein (acnegenic).
Sports nutritionAdolescent athletes: higher protein needs (1.4–1.7g/kg), ↑ calcium (bone mass accrual peak), ↑ iron (adolescent girls). Screen for: RED-S (relative energy deficiency).Protein: 1.4–1.7g/kg. Calcium 1300mg/day (adolescent peak bone mass). Iron 18mg (girls with heavy periods + sport). Magnesium (sleep + muscle function + cramping). Creatine: generally safe from age 18 (limited data under 18). Avoid: unregulated supplements, stimulants, protein bars with undisclosed additives.
SleepNatural circadian delay in puberty — biologically normal to not feel tired until midnight. Social jetlag is epidemic in adolescents.Education (NOT lectures) about sleep importance. Melatonin 0.5mg (phase advance — given 5–6hrs before desired sleep time). Blue-light glasses from 8pm. Consistent weekend wake time (within 1 hour of weekday). Later school start time advocacy.
Digital wellnessAverage Indian adolescent: 6–8 hours daily screen time. Displaced: sleep, exercise, in-person social interaction, creativity.Tech boundaries: no phone in bedroom, phone off at 9pm. Monotasking (one screen at a time). "Dopamine fast" — 24hr break from screens weekly. Offline hobbies prescription. Parental modelling is key.
